zones.name: Analysis Pipeline Core
zones.description: Orchestrates the multi-phase codebase analysis pipeline including enrichment, context generation, zone detection output, and LLM integration.
insights: 16 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports
16 entry points signal this zone is the primary public API surface for the sourcevision analysis engine — a high-value integration point worth documenting carefully.
The presence of a dedicated claude-client.ts gateway within this zone follows the project's gateway pattern, keeping LLM vendor coupling isolated.

Bidirectional imports with both 'unit' and 'analyzers-2' zones (37 in, 8 out to unit; 7 out to analyzers-2) suggest some coupling pressure — the zone boundary between pipeline orchestration and core algorithms could be made more directional.
Strong cohesion (0.67) with a well-defined gateway for Claude API access — this is the correct pattern for vendor isolation.
Bidirectional imports with the 'unit' zone (37 imports in, 8 out) indicate the algorithmic boundary is not fully one-directional; extracting shared types into 'unit' could reduce this tension.
pdf-report.ts and llms-txt.ts as entry points from within the same zone suggests output generation is a first-class concern — appropriate given the pipeline architecture.
Zone "analysis-pipeline" has files across 9 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory
Imports 2 files from the e2e test zone — production code depending on test infrastructure inverts the dependency direction and must be eliminated
73 outgoing runtime calls into cli-interface means the pipeline layer drives CLI output directly rather than being consumed by it; output formatting logic has leaked upward into the pipeline
analysis-pipeline imports 2 files from the e2e zone — production code must not depend on test infrastructure; shared helpers should be extracted to a non-test location

73 runtime calls flow from analysis-pipeline into cli-interface, inverting the expected consumer relationship; the pipeline should be consumed by CLI, not call into it — output formatting concerns have leaked into the pipeline layer

The AI enrichment sub-system (claude-client.ts, enrich-batch.ts, enrich-config.ts, completion-reader.ts) forms a coherent cluster within this zone but has no clear boundary from the rest of the pipeline — extracting it as an 'ai-enrichment' sub-zone would make the LLM dependency surface explicit and independently testable.

The project already provides a dedicated llm-client package and claude-client package for vendor-neutral LLM access; src/analyzers/claude-client.ts creates a second, parallel LLM gateway within sourcevision that bypasses the shared foundation layer, violating the four-tier dependency hierarchy.

src/analyzers/claude-client.ts duplicates LLM vendor coupling that is already handled by the @n-dx/llm-client and @n-dx/claude-client packages; this second gateway creates an unsanctioned shortcut from the domain layer directly to the Anthropic SDK, bypassing the foundation layer that exists specifically to isolate vendor coupling.

The project's public.ts convention (one explicit public API per package) has no intra-zone analog for this zone's 16 entry points; an src/analyzers/public.ts barrel applying the same convention internally would gate accidental API surface growth and make intentional exports visible during code review — the convention already exists in the codebase but is not applied at zone granularity.

completion-reader.ts breaks the enrich-* naming pattern established by its siblings enrich-batch.ts and enrich-config.ts; renaming it to enrich-completion-reader.ts or enrich-reader.ts would make the AI enrichment subsystem identifiable by a name-prefix scan, matching the implicit naming cluster these three files form.

Add an src/analyzers/public.ts barrel as the single declared export surface for this zone, mirroring the package-level public.ts convention already used across all five packages; this caps the 16 entry points at a defined gate and makes undeclared cross-zone imports visible as convention violations.

Rename completion-reader.ts to enrich-reader.ts (or enrich-completion.ts) to complete the enrich-* naming cluster with enrich-batch.ts and enrich-config.ts; the current name breaks the discoverable naming pattern and obscures that these three files form a coherent AI enrichment subsystem.
[call graph] 997 internal calls, 93 outgoing, 208 incoming (cohesion: 0.91, coupling: 0.09)

zones.name: Claude Code Configuration
zones.description: Stores Claude Code editor settings and local overrides for the development environment.
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Settings are split into tracked and local files, following a clean gitignore pattern for developer-specific overrides.
No entry points or cross-zone imports indicate this zone is purely configuration with no runtime role.
Two-file zone with perfect cohesion and zero coupling — correctly isolated from all runtime code.

zones.name: CLI Interface & Command Layer
zones.description: Implements all user-facing CLI commands, output formatting, error handling, and manifest management for the sourcevision tool.
insights: The presence of git-credential-helper.ts and prd-epic-resolver.ts alongside standard init/analyze commands suggests the CLI has grown beyond pure UX into domain orchestration — these concerns could be extracted.
Strong cohesion (0.71) with contained coupling (0.29) makes this the most architecturally clean zone in the batch.

branch-work-collector.ts being classified here (alongside manifest.ts) suggests data collection for git-branching workflows is treated as a CLI-tier concern rather than a core algorithm — a reasonable design choice if this data is only ever surfaced via CLI.
Zone "cli-interface" has files across 7 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory
Highest cohesion and lowest coupling in the batch — this zone has the cleanest architectural boundary of any analyzed zone.

prd-epic-resolver.ts in the CLI layer implies PRD integration logic lives here rather than in a domain layer; if rex integration grows, a gateway module for rex imports should be added following the project's gateway pattern.
cli/errors.ts and cli/output.ts as distinct entry points indicate error handling and output formatting are properly separated from command logic.

Receives 73 incoming calls from analysis-pipeline and 15 from core-algorithms despite being classified as a leaf consumer — the CLI layer is functioning as a shared output-formatting utility module, not a pure terminal consumer
High cohesion (0.71) and low coupling (0.29) metrics mask the inbound call pressure: both upstream zones actively call into cli-interface, meaning it is a de facto shared library for output concerns

cli-interface is called by both analysis-pipeline (73 calls) and core-algorithms (15 calls), making it a shared output utility rather than a pure terminal consumer; output formatting logic should be extracted into a dedicated shared module to restore the leaf-consumer role

src/analyzers/manifest.ts is an analyzer-domain file (manages analysis output metadata) classified in the CLI layer; manifest lifecycle is a pipeline/domain concern and its placement in cli-interface leaks domain logic into the presentation tier.

branch-work-collector.ts lives here while its three sibling files (classifier, filter, store) live in core-algorithms; the collector is the data-acquisition half of the branch-work feature and its separation from the processing half (classifier/filter) means the feature cannot be understood or refactored without crossing a zone boundary.

src/analyzers/manifest.ts manages analysis manifest metadata — a domain/pipeline concern — but is classified in the CLI interface zone; this mixes domain state management with presentation logic and should be moved to analysis-pipeline or core-algorithms.

branch-work-collector.ts is separated from its sibling branch-work-classifier.ts, branch-work-filter.ts, and branch-work-store.ts which reside in core-algorithms; a four-file feature cluster split across two zones creates unnecessary coupling and makes the feature impossible to relocate without touching both zones.

The 12 analyzers→cli imports and 4 unit→cli imports almost certainly target a small subset of cli exports (likely cli/output.ts and cli/errors.ts based on their entry-point status); the concrete refactoring is not to move files but to extract the imported symbols into a new src/shared/output.ts and src/shared/errors.ts, then update cli/output.ts and cli/errors.ts to re-export from shared/ — this makes CLI a true leaf consumer while giving upstream zones a legitimate import target without changing any runtime behavior.

Before: analysis-pipeline and core-algorithms import directly from cli/output.ts and cli/errors.ts. After: create src/shared/output.ts and src/shared/errors.ts containing the exported symbols; cli/output.ts and cli/errors.ts re-export from src/shared/; upstream zones import from src/shared/ — CLI remains a leaf consumer with zero inbound production imports and the 17 upward dependency edges become legitimate downward imports.
[call graph] 362 internal calls, 5 outgoing, 88 incoming (cohesion: 0.99, coupling: 0.01)

zones.name: Core Algorithms & Schema
zones.description: Houses the foundational algorithmic components — Louvain community detection, zone computation, branch classification, risk scoring, and the canonical schema definitions.
insights: Louvain.ts living here alongside zones.ts and schema/v1.ts is architecturally sound — community detection and its output schema should co-evolve.
The branch-work-* triple (classifier, filter, store) shows a clean local MVC-like separation within this zone.
Imports from both 'analyzers' and 'cli' zones (8 in from analyzers, 4 in from unit→cli) suggest this zone serves as a true shared foundation — the most critical zone to keep stable.
Zone "core-algorithms" has files across 11 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory
Zone anchors the schema (v1.ts, data-files.ts) alongside the Louvain algorithm — this tight coupling between algorithm and its output schema is intentional and appropriate.

Moderate cohesion (0.56) with 8 files importing back into 'analyzers' suggests some upward dependency leakage; auditing whether any analyzer-specific logic has drifted into this foundational zone would improve layering.
cli/mcp.ts appearing as an entry point in this zone is unexpected — MCP transport concerns may have leaked into the algorithmic layer.
Imports 4 files from cli-interface — foundational algorithm code depending on the CLI presentation layer is a strict violation of the four-tier hierarchy defined in CLAUDE.md
Imports 2 files from the e2e test zone — production algorithm code must not depend on test infrastructure

200 runtime calls into analysis-pipeline across only 37 import statements (~5.4 calls per import) indicates extremely high per-import utilization; these imports are load-bearing and any decoupling effort will have outsized cascading impact

core-algorithms (unit) imports from cli-interface (4 imports, 15 calls) — foundational algorithms must not depend on CLI presentation code; this directly breaks the layered dependency hierarchy in CLAUDE.md
core-algorithms imports 2 files from the e2e test zone — production code must not depend on test infrastructure

src/cli/commands/workspace.ts is a CLI command file classified inside the core-algorithms (unit) zone — a CLI presentation artifact embedded in the foundational algorithm layer, directly contradicting the four-tier hierarchy.

cli/mcp.ts appearing as an entry point in this zone means MCP transport registration code — an orchestration-tier concern — lives inside the algorithm foundation; this is a stronger layering violation than the known insight describes because MCP is orchestration, not domain.

The branch-work cluster is split across two zones: classifier, filter, and store live in core-algorithms while collector lives in cli-interface; a single feature's four files spanning two zones prevents the cluster from evolving as a unit and forces consumers to import from both zones.

src/cli/commands/workspace.ts is a CLI command file resident in the core-algorithms zone; CLI command files must not appear in the foundational algorithm layer — this file should be reclassified into cli-interface.

src/cli/mcp.ts (MCP transport registration) is classified as an entry point within core-algorithms; MCP server setup is an orchestration-tier concern and its presence in the algorithm foundation layer violates the four-tier dependency hierarchy.

core-algorithms scores the worst fragility combination in the package: lowest cohesion (0.56) AND highest coupling (0.44) simultaneously — by the risk framework this is the single most fragile zone and the highest-priority stabilization target before any refactoring elsewhere.

The zone name 'core-algorithms' actively misrepresents its contents: it contains a state store (branch-work-store.ts), a CLI command (workspace.ts), and MCP transport registration (mcp.ts) alongside actual algorithm files; a reviewer scanning the filesystem would not identify misclassified files because the name creates a false expectation of pure algorithm content.

core-algorithms has the worst cohesion/coupling ratio in the package (0.56 cohesion, 0.44 coupling) — it is the highest-fragility zone by metric and should be the primary stabilization target; all other refactoring work risks increasing this zone's instability further until its boundary is fixed.

Rename the zone to reflect its actual mixed content (e.g., 'algorithm-foundation') and enforce the name in architecture docs; the current name 'core-algorithms' causes reviewers to overlook misclassified non-algorithm files (workspace.ts, mcp.ts, branch-work-store.ts) because the name implies homogeneous algorithmic content.
[call graph] 2161 internal calls, 236 outgoing, 10 incoming (cohesion: 0.9, coupling: 0.1)

zones.name: Integration & Validation Tests
zones.description: Cross-cutting test zone grouping schema validation logic with e2e CLI tests and integration pipeline tests that exercise the full analysis workflow end-to-end.
insights: High coupling (0.57) — 3 imports target "static-analyzer-services"

The co-location of src/schema/validate.ts (a source file) with test files is a sign that the community detection algorithm found tight structural coupling between the validator and the tests that exercise it
e2e and integration tests are grouped together rather than separated by test type, which may make it harder to run targeted test subsets
Low cohesion (0.43) and moderate coupling (0.57) suggest this is an algorithmically-detected convenience grouping rather than a true architectural zone

Cohesion of 0.43 is below the 0.4 threshold and coupling of 0.57 is approaching the warning boundary — this zone mixes e2e, integration, and unit test files with a source module, indicating it is not a semantically coherent zone.

src/schema/validate.ts being pulled into the test zone rather than the schema zone suggests it is heavily imported by tests and less so by production code paths — verify it is not test-only logic living in the source tree.

cli-analyze and cli-serve e2e tests belonging to the same zone as integration pipeline tests implies the CLI and pipeline are tightly coupled at the test boundary, which is expected for a CLI tool but worth monitoring as test suites grow.

The cross-zone import data shows 'analyzers → e2e: 2 imports' and 'unit → e2e: 2 imports' — production source zones importing from what resolves to the e2e/integration test zone; this is a critical boundary violation where runtime code depends on test infrastructure.

The e2e sub-zone participates in four bidirectional exchanges (unit↔e2e, analyzers↔e2e, e2e→analyzers-2) making it an unintended hub rather than a pure test-leaf, which prevents clean test-only CI filtering.

Production zones (analyzers, unit) import from the e2e/integration test zone (2 imports each) — test infrastructure is leaking into the runtime dependency graph, which can cause test-only code to be bundled into production artifacts.

The e2e zone forms bidirectional import pairs with unit, analyzers, and analyzers-2 simultaneously, making it a cross-cutting hub rather than a terminal test consumer — test zones should have zero inbound imports from source zones.

src/schema/validate.ts residing in the integration-validation-tests zone means the community detection algorithm found its heaviest structural coupling is to test files rather than to production schema consumers — this is a signal that validate.ts may contain test-only validation logic that should either be moved to src/test-utils/ or refactored so its production path is consumed by more source modules

The co-presence of e2e, integration, and unit test files in one zone alongside a production source file (src/schema/validate.ts) means this zone simultaneously violates the test-type separation principle and the source/test separation principle — it conflates two independent concerns in one grouping

src/schema/validate.ts is a production source file that the community detection algorithm placed in a test zone because its heaviest import consumers are test files; if this module contains production logic it must be moved to a source zone (e.g. src/schema/) so it is never accidentally excluded from production builds or included only in test-scoped bundles

This zone simultaneously satisfies BOTH conditions of the fragile-zone definition: cohesion 0.43 (below 0.5 threshold) AND coupling 0.57 (above 0.5 threshold) — it is the only zone in the entire package that is definitively fragile by both criteria at once, making it the highest-priority structural cleanup target among the three zones in this batch.

The zone name 'integration-validation-tests' omits the e2e and unit test files it also contains; a more honest name would expose the naming problem and motivate splitting — the misleading name is actively obscuring the zone's mixed nature from anyone navigating by zone ID alone.

This is the only zone in the package that is fragile by both metrics simultaneously (cohesion 0.43 AND coupling 0.57). It should be treated as the highest-priority zone restructuring target: move src/schema/validate.ts to its correct source zone and separate e2e, integration, and unit test files into type-appropriate zones to drive both metrics toward the test-fixture-projects profile (cohesion 1.0, coupling 0.0).

Zone name 'integration-validation-tests' actively conceals that the zone also contains e2e and unit test files plus a production source module. Rename to something like 'mixed-test-zone' or split it — the misleading name is an obstacle to architectural remediation because it makes the zone appear coherent in documentation while the metrics show the opposite.
[call graph] 87 internal calls, 10 outgoing, 11 incoming (cohesion: 0.9, coupling: 0.1)

zones.name: Static Analyzer Services
zones.description: Individual analyzer service implementations (callgraph, imports, inventory, components, route detection) and their path utilities, co-located with corresponding unit tests.
insights: Bidirectional imports with the Analysis Pipeline Core zone (15 out, 7 in) suggest these files are tightly coupled to the orchestration layer and may belong in the same zone
The route-detection.ts file classified as route-handler is semantically a detection utility, not a route handler — the archetype classification may be misleading
src/util/paths.ts is listed as an entry point but is a utility module; it likely belongs in the foundation layer rather than as a primary surface of this zone

High cross-zone import density with Analysis Pipeline Core (22 total cross-imports) indicates this zone and 'analyzers' may have been split by the community detection algorithm despite being a single cohesive domain; consider whether they should be merged.

Test files are co-located in the same detected zone as their source modules, which reflects tight structural coupling between implementations and tests — good for discoverability but means test changes can affect zone metrics.

route-detection.ts is classified as 'route-handler' by the archetype system but its role is static analysis of routes in user codebases, not request handling — the archetype override should be set to 'utility' or 'service'.

The single import from this zone into the cli zone (analyzers-2 → cli: 1 import) is a dependency inversion — a domain service layer must not import from the terminal consumer layer; this is the one structural violation in an otherwise correctly-oriented package.
static-analyzer-services imports from the cli zone (1 import), inverting the intended dependency direction; cli should only consume from this zone, not the reverse.

src/util/paths.ts has no semantic relationship to static analysis algorithms — it is a filesystem path utility consumed across multiple zones and should live in a shared foundation layer, not inside the analyzer services zone where it creates an artificial coupling point for any zone needing path utilities

The analyzers → cli cross-zone edge carries 12 imports — more than the analyzers-2 → cli edge (1 import) and the unit → cli edge (4 imports) combined — yet the 12-import inversion from the Analysis Pipeline Core into the CLI layer is the most severe individual dependency inversion in the package and has not been called out at commensurate severity

src/util/paths.ts is a general-purpose filesystem utility that is imported across multiple zones; housing it inside the static-analyzer-services zone forces every consumer to take a dependency on that zone solely to access path helpers — extract it to a dedicated src/util/ or src/foundation/ layer with no zone affiliation

The zone name 'static-analyzer-services' uses a 'services' suffix that conventionally implies injectable, stateful, or lifecycle-managed objects; the actual files are pure stateless analysis functions — 'static-analyzer-utilities' or 'static-analyzers' would more accurately reflect the contents and avoid implying a services architecture that does not exist.

With cohesion 0.54 and coupling 0.46, this zone is approaching the fragile threshold (cohesion < 0.5, coupling > 0.5) from both directions simultaneously; any addition of a misplaced file (e.g. another shared utility like paths.ts) could push it over the boundary into the high-coupling/low-cohesion fragile quadrant.

Zone name 'static-analyzer-services' misleads — the suffix 'services' implies injectable or stateful objects, but all files are pure stateless analyzer functions. Rename to 'static-analyzers' or 'analyzer-utilities' to match actual content and avoid implying a service-layer pattern that does not exist.

Cohesion 0.54 / coupling 0.46 puts this zone one misplaced file away from the fragile quadrant (cohesion < 0.5 AND coupling > 0.5). Extracting src/util/paths.ts to a shared foundation layer (already recommended) will move both metrics in the right direction before the zone degrades further.
[call graph] 384 internal calls, 7 outgoing, 34 incoming (cohesion: 0.98, coupling: 0.02)

zones.name: Test Fixture Projects
zones.description: Self-contained sample codebases (a Remix app and a small TypeScript project) used as ground-truth inputs for e2e and integration tests of the analysis pipeline.
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Perfect cohesion (1.0) and zero coupling confirm these files are fully isolated from production code, which is the correct design for test fixtures
Two distinct fixture projects (remix-app and small-ts-project) provide coverage of different framework conventions — adding a plain JavaScript project would complete the framework diversity matrix
Fixture files have no entry points, meaning they are never imported by production code; any accidental import from a non-test file should be treated as a critical architecture violation
Zero coupling to any other zone is the ideal state for test fixtures — these files cannot accidentally influence production bundle size or introduce transitive dependencies.
The Remix app fixture covers nested route conventions (_auth layout, dynamic segments, index routes) which is well-suited for validating route-detection.ts accuracy across real-world patterns.
Fixture projects should be kept minimal and representative; monitor that they do not grow into large, hard-to-maintain synthetic codebases that drift from real-world usage patterns over time.
Zone "test-fixture-projects" has files across 5 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory
test-fixture-projects is the only zone in the entire package achieving the ideal dependency profile (cohesion 1.0, coupling 0.0) and serves as the reference target state for the other test zones.
Perfect isolation (cohesion 1.0, coupling 0.0) makes this zone the architectural gold standard within the package — the integration-validation-tests zone should be refactored toward this profile.

The two fixture projects (remix-app, small-ts-project) cover only TypeScript-specific patterns; there is no plain JavaScript fixture, no CommonJS fixture, and no alternative framework (Vue, Angular, Next.js) fixture — the route-detection and component analysis code may have untested edge cases for non-TypeScript or non-Remix projects despite appearing fully covered by cohesion metrics.

The perfect-isolation profile of this zone (cohesion 1.0, coupling 0.0) should be codified as the explicit architectural target for all test zones in a contribution guideline or ADR; currently it is an implicit emergent property with no documented enforcement mechanism, meaning future test additions could degrade it without anyone noticing.

Fixture coverage gap: both existing fixtures (remix-app, small-ts-project) are TypeScript-only. Add a plain JavaScript fixture project to ensure inventory, import, and zone detection code paths are exercised against JS files without TypeScript-specific syntax, since the analyzers must support both.

The perfect isolation of this zone (cohesion 1.0, coupling 0.0) is an implicit convention with no enforcement. Document it as the explicit target state for all test zones in PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md or an ADR so that accidental imports from/into fixture files trigger a documented rule violation rather than a silent metric regression.
[call graph] 5 internal calls, 0 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)

zones.name: Workspace Root & Documentation
zones.description: Top-level monorepo scaffolding including documentation, build configuration, and workspace metadata.
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Architecture and design docs (ARCHITECTURE.md, WORKSPACE_DESIGN.md) live here alongside build config, making project intent discoverable at the root.
No entry points and zero coupling confirm this zone has no runtime dependencies — it's purely structural.
PNG assets (SourceVision-F.png, SourceVision.png) co-located with docs suggest these are documentation illustrations, not UI assets.
Zero coupling and perfect cohesion make this zone a clean separation between project metadata and runtime packages.
Image assets mixed with markdown docs and build config — consider moving visual assets to a dedicated docs/ folder for cleaner separation.
Hub: src/analyzers/zones.ts is imported by 3 zones — cross-cutting dependency
Hub: src/schema/index.ts is imported by 3 zones — cross-cutting dependency
Hub: src/schema/v1.ts is imported by 3 zones — cross-cutting dependency
Bidirectional coupling: "analysis-pipeline" ↔ "core-algorithms" (8+37 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Bidirectional coupling: "analysis-pipeline" ↔ "static-analyzer-services" (7+15 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Bidirectional coupling: "analysis-pipeline" ↔ "cli-interface" (12+5 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Bidirectional coupling: "cli-interface" ↔ "core-algorithms" (8+4 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Bidirectional coupling: "core-algorithms" ↔ "integration-validation-tests" (2+2 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Bidirectional coupling: "core-algorithms" ↔ "static-analyzer-services" (2+2 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Size imbalance: largest zone has 39 files vs smallest with 2 — uneven decomposition
2 circular dependency chains detected — see imports.json for details

The heaviest cross-zone flow (unit → analyzers: 37 imports) runs upward from the algorithmic foundation into the pipeline — combined with the reverse flow (analyzers → unit: 8), this bidirectionality is the primary coupling risk in the codebase.

The analyzers-2 zone (not in this batch) participates in a three-way exchange with both 'analyzers' and 'unit', suggesting the split between analyzers and analyzers-2 may be an artifact of community detection rather than a meaningful architectural boundary worth preserving.

The cli zone's low coupling (0.29) and high cohesion (0.71) compared to 'analyzers' (0.33 coupling) reinforces that the dependency gradient is correctly oriented: CLI is a leaf consumer, not a shared dependency.
No zone in this batch has coupling above 0.44 — the overall architecture maintains healthy isolation between concerns, with the unit↔analyzers bidirectionality being the main area to watch.

The bidirectional import relationship between 'analyzers' (Analysis Pipeline Core) and 'analyzers-2' (Static Analyzer Services) — 7 imports in one direction and 15 in the other — is the strongest cross-zone coupling signal in this package and suggests the Louvain algorithm split what is functionally a single module cluster; a merge or explicit boundary enforcement would improve architectural clarity

The test zones (integration-validation-tests, test-fixture-projects) together account for roughly half the zones in this batch, reflecting a healthy test investment, but the mixing of e2e, integration, and unit files in a single detected zone suggests the import graph does not cleanly separate test types — this can make targeted CI runs harder to configure

The overall dependency flow (fixtures → e2e ← unit → analyzers → cli) forms a clean acyclic graph when the bidirectional analyzers/analyzers-2 coupling is resolved, indicating the package architecture is fundamentally sound with one actionable cleanup opportunity

The four runtime zones form a recognizable layered architecture: schema/algorithms (unit) → pipeline (analyzers, analyzers-2) → CLI (cli), with the bidirectional unit↔analyzers link being the one deviation from strict layering.
The .claude and root zones are correctly inert — zero coupling means editor configuration and documentation cannot accidentally affect runtime behavior.

With 16 entry points, the 'analyzers' zone has a large public surface; a dedicated public.ts barrel (following the project's package convention) would make intentional exports explicit and prevent accidental API surface growth.

Two of the five source zones (analyzers and analyzers-2) have significant bidirectional imports totaling 22 cross-imports, making them the dominant coupling hotspot in the package; resolving this by consolidating or drawing an explicit interface boundary would raise the average cohesion score meaningfully.

The CLI Interface zone has outbound imports to both analyzer zones and the unit/schema zone but receives very few inbound imports, consistent with its role as a terminal consumer — this is a healthy architectural pattern for a CLI entry layer.

Test fixture files (cohesion 1.0, coupling 0) and the Claude Code configuration zone (cohesion 1.0) represent the two cleanest zones in the package; the production source zones could target similar isolation by reducing the bidirectional coupling between analyzer layers.
src/analyzers/ contains files from 4 zones (analysis-pipeline, cli-interface, core-algorithms, static-analyzer-services) — consider grouping into subdirectories to clarify architectural boundaries

Three-zone circular dependency exists: core-algorithms (unit) → analysis-pipeline (analyzers) → analyzers-2 → core-algorithms (unit) — the 2 return imports from analyzers-2→unit close this cycle and represent a critical architectural violation

Two distinct production-imports-test violations exist (analysis-pipeline→e2e and core-algorithms→e2e); the shared code should be extracted to src/test-utils/ or a comparable non-test location accessible by both production and test code

The cli-interface zone is called into by every other runtime zone (15 + 73 inbound calls), meaning it acts as a hub for output formatting despite having the cleanest cohesion/coupling metrics — metric hygiene is masking a real structural responsibility

The 200-call edge from core-algorithms→analysis-pipeline is the single largest call graph edge in the package by a wide margin; it represents the highest-risk refactoring surface and any decoupling work must address this volume first

unit→cli (4 imports) directly contradicts the four-tier hierarchy documented in CLAUDE.md where domain/algorithm layers must not import from the orchestration/CLI tier — this is a documented architectural rule violation

The package has two classes of architectural violation: (1) a single dependency inversion (analyzers-2 → cli) in the source layer, and (2) bidirectional source↔test coupling (analyzers/unit ↔ e2e) in the test layer — both are distinct root causes requiring separate fixes.

The clean acyclic flow (fixtures → e2e → analyzers-2/unit → analyzers → cli) breaks down specifically at the source→e2e edges; removing those 4 imports would make the entire import graph a proper DAG with correct directional flow.

The two confirmed circular dependency chains flagged in imports.json likely involve the e2e zone given it participates in the most bidirectional exchanges; resolving the source→test imports is the highest-leverage single action to eliminate circularity.

The overall package obeys a four-layer hierarchy (fixtures < tests < domain < cli) with exactly two violations (one inversion, one source-imports-test pair) — the architecture is structurally sound and the remediation surface is narrow.

Three-zone circular import cycle detected: core-algorithms → analysis-pipeline → analyzers-2 → core-algorithms; circular zone-level dependencies prevent clean layering and must be broken by extracting the shared interface
Production code in two zones imports from the e2e test zone; shared test helpers should be moved to a non-test package (e.g., src/test-utils/) to eliminate production→test dependencies

cli-interface functions as a shared output-formatting hub (88 total inbound calls across 2 upstream zones) despite being a leaf consumer by classification; a dedicated output-utilities zone should be extracted to make this responsibility explicit

Source zones importing from test zones (analyzers → e2e: 2, unit → e2e: 2) combined with the confirmed circular dependency count (2) strongly suggests these cross-boundary edges are the circular dependency chains — fixing them resolves both the layering violation and the circularity in one change.

Excluding the two violations, all cross-zone import edges flow strictly downward (fixtures → test → domain → cli), confirming the intended layered architecture is largely intact and the codebase is close to a fully clean dependency graph.
unit→cli (4 imports) violates the four-tier dependency hierarchy defined in CLAUDE.md — foundational algorithm code must not import from the CLI presentation layer

The project's own gateway pattern (one gateway file per upstream package, re-export only) is not applied within the sourcevision package itself: src/analyzers/claude-client.ts is an informal intra-package gateway that breaks the foundation-layer contract by importing the Anthropic SDK directly rather than routing through @n-dx/llm-client.

The three confirmed layering violations (unit→cli, production→e2e×2) and the one transport misplacement (mcp.ts in core-algorithms) share a common root cause: the src/analyzers/ and src/cli/ directories commingle files from four different architectural zones with no subdirectory structure to enforce boundaries; adding subdirectories (e.g. src/analyzers/enrichment/, src/analyzers/branch-work/) would make zone membership legible from the filesystem alone.

The branch-work feature (collector + classifier + filter + store) is the only multi-file domain feature in this package that is structurally split across zones; every other multi-file concern (louvain+zones, enrich-batch+enrich-config, etc.) co-locates in a single zone, making branch-work an outlier that signals the zone boundary between core-algorithms and cli-interface is drawn at the wrong seam for this feature.

All three non-CLI production zones import from the CLI zone: analyzers (12 imports), unit (4 imports), and analyzers-2 (1 import), for a total of 17 upward dependency edges. CLI is functioning as a shared utility library for output formatting and error handling despite being classified as the terminal consumer layer — the shared CLI utilities should be extracted to a src/output/ or src/ui/ foundation module so the CLI zone truly has zero inbound source dependencies

The analyzers → cli direction carries 12 imports, making it the single largest dependency inversion in the package by import count — three times larger than unit → cli (4) and twelve times larger than analyzers-2 → cli (1). Prior findings call out unit → cli and analyzers-2 → cli as violations but treat them as isolated; the full picture is that 17 cross-zone imports flow upward into CLI, constituting a systemic pattern rather than isolated mistakes, and the root cause is almost certainly shared output-formatting or error-type definitions living in the CLI package that should be in a lower layer

src/util/paths.ts (in static-analyzer-services) and src/schema/validate.ts (in integration-validation-tests) are both misplaced production source files that the community detection algorithm assigned to non-natural zones due to their import gravity; extracting both to appropriately-named source zones would increase zone cohesion scores across the board and eliminate two of the structural anomalies without touching any runtime behavior

The package has exactly three categories of architectural violation: (1) upward source-layer→CLI imports (17 total, three zones); (2) source-layer→test-layer imports (4 total, two zones); (3) a three-zone circular dependency closing through the analyzers-2→unit return edge. These three categories are independent root causes. Fixing only the CLI inversion (category 1) eliminates 17 bad edges and likely the largest call-graph coupling surface in one change, making it the highest-leverage first action.

src/analyzers/claude-client.ts bypasses @n-dx/llm-client (the designated foundation-layer vendor abstraction) and imports the Anthropic SDK directly from within the domain layer; this creates a second untracked LLM vendor coupling path and undermines the architectural guarantee that vendor lock-in is isolated to the foundation layer.

The src/analyzers/ directory contains files from four distinct architectural zones (analysis-pipeline, core-algorithms, cli-interface, analyzers-2) with no subdirectory structure; without filesystem-level zone separation, zone membership is only discoverable via tooling and accidental cross-zone imports are invisible during code review.

The CLI zone receives 17 upward imports from three separate production zones (analyzers: 12, unit: 4, analyzers-2: 1), making it a de facto shared library rather than a leaf consumer. Any code in the CLI zone imported by upstream zones must be extracted to a lower-tier foundation module (e.g. src/output/, src/errors/) to restore the four-tier hierarchy documented in CLAUDE.md

analyzers → cli carries 12 imports — the single largest dependency inversion in the package — yet it has not been flagged at critical severity in prior enrichment passes. These 12 imports almost certainly represent shared output-formatting helpers or error types that belong in a foundation layer; until moved, every change to those CLI utilities risks breaking the analysis pipeline

The 'analyzers-2' zone (11 files, cohesion 0.54, part of the three-zone circular dependency) has a Louvain-artifact name with no semantic meaning; without an intentional name in architecture docs, every enrichment pass treats it as an anonymous zone and its role in the circular dependency goes undescribed — assigning it a permanent name (e.g., 'static-analyzer-services') and documenting it in ARCHITECTURE.md would make the circular dependency chain legible to reviewers without tooling.

The analysis-pipeline ↔ core-algorithms bidirectional coupling (37+8=45 total cross-imports) has no shared interface definition mediating the boundary; both zones exchange types and functions through direct file imports, meaning there is no single place to see what the contract between them is. An explicit src/analyzers/pipeline-contract.ts interface file would make the boundary legible and prevent further cross-imports from silently widening the coupling surface — this is the intra-package equivalent of the gateway pattern the project already enforces for cross-package imports.

The three categories of architectural violation (17 CLI inversions, 4 production→test imports, 1 three-zone circular dependency) are currently tracked only in enrichment insights and not in any checked-in document; adding an ARCHITECTURE_VIOLATIONS.md listing each known violation with owner and resolution status would prevent new violations from being introduced by contributors who haven't read the full enrichment history, and would let CI tooling eventually gate on the list shrinking rather than growing.

No zone in the package has an explicit 'do not import from' guard — the four-tier hierarchy in CLAUDE.md is documented prose but has no machine-enforceable form; adding an eslint-plugin-import or dependency-cruiser rule set to package.json that encodes the allowed import directions would turn the 17 known violations into lint errors and prevent regression after each is fixed.

The three zones in this batch form a severity gradient by zone-health metrics: test-fixture-projects (cohesion 1.0, coupling 0.0) is the gold standard, static-analyzer-services (0.54 / 0.46) is borderline, and integration-validation-tests (0.43 / 0.57) is definitively fragile — yet remediation effort has been spread evenly across all three in prior passes rather than being concentrated on the one zone that is objectively broken by both criteria.

The analyzers-2 → cli import (1 import) is the smallest dependency inversion in the entire package by count and is almost certainly a single mis-placed import or a one-line type reference; it should be the first inversion resolved because it can likely be eliminated in under 10 minutes with zero risk of regression, establishing a clean precedent before tackling the 12-import and 4-import inversions.

Zone ID naming is inconsistent across the package: some zones use short single-word IDs ('unit', 'cli', 'analyzers') while others use verbose multi-word hyphenated IDs ('static-analyzer-services', 'integration-validation-tests', 'test-fixture-projects'). This inconsistency makes zone references in tooling output, documentation, and config files harder to predict — a naming convention (e.g. always use kebab-case noun phrases without category suffixes like '-tests' or '-services') should be applied uniformly.

The package has no zone whose sole responsibility is shared test infrastructure (helpers, factories, mocks). The absence of a dedicated 'test-utils' or 'test-support' zone is the structural root cause of two separate findings: production code importing from test zones (to access shared helpers) and src/schema/validate.ts being gravitationally pulled into a test zone. Creating this zone would resolve both issues in a single structural change.

The three production-layer zones (static-analyzer-services / analyzers / unit) all have coupling scores between 0.33 and 0.57 — none achieves the low-coupling profile of the CLI zone (0.29) or the fixture zone (0.0). The entire production layer is structurally more coupled than either the CLI leaf or the test fixtures, which inverts the expected coupling gradient: foundation layers should be less coupled than consumer layers, not more.

The implicit enrich-* naming cluster (enrich-batch.ts, enrich-config.ts) combined with the AI enrichment subsystem being the only identified cluster without a subdirectory strongly suggests src/analyzers/enrichment/ should be the first subdirectory created when adding filesystem zone boundaries; it would immediately consolidate 3-4 files, reduce the appearance of the analysis-pipeline zone's file count, and establish the subdirectory pattern for the more complex branch-work/ and output/ extractions to follow.

The gateway pattern (one gateway file per upstream package, re-export only) is applied at cross-package boundaries but has no intra-package equivalent for the analysis-pipeline ↔ core-algorithms interface; applying the same pattern internally — a src/analyzers/pipeline-contract.ts that re-exports only the types and functions crossing this specific boundary — would make the 45-import coupling surface explicit, auditable, and bounded in exactly the same way the project already manages cross-package coupling.

No zone serves as a dedicated shared test infrastructure zone ('test-utils'). This single structural gap is the common root cause of two separately-flagged violations: production zones importing from test zones to access shared helpers, and src/schema/validate.ts being assigned to a test zone by community detection. Adding a src/test-utils/ source directory with its own zone would resolve both without touching any production runtime behavior.

Zone naming is inconsistent: 'unit', 'cli', 'analyzers' use short-form IDs while 'static-analyzer-services', 'integration-validation-tests', 'test-fixture-projects' use verbose hyphenated IDs. Standardize on one convention (kebab-case noun phrases without type suffixes like '-tests' or '-services') and update zone IDs in sourcevision config so tooling output and cross-references are predictable.

The production-layer zones (static-analyzer-services: 0.46, analyzers: 0.33, unit: varying) are more coupled than the CLI leaf layer (0.29) — the coupling gradient is inverted from the expected pattern where foundation layers have lower coupling than consumer layers. This inversion is a systemic signal that the foundation/domain zones are over-importing from each other rather than depending on a shared abstraction layer below them.

Test fixture files (cohesion 1.0, coupling 0) and the Claude Code configuration zone (cohesion 1.0) represent the two cleanest zones in the package; the production source zones could target similar isolation by reducing the bidirectional coupling between analyzer layers.
The .claude and root zones are correctly inert — zero coupling means editor configuration and documentation cannot accidentally affect runtime behavior.

The CLI Interface zone has outbound imports to both analyzer zones and the unit/schema zone but receives very few inbound imports, consistent with its role as a terminal consumer — this is a healthy architectural pattern for a CLI entry layer.

The four runtime zones form a recognizable layered architecture: schema/algorithms (unit) → pipeline (analyzers, analyzers-2) → CLI (cli), with the bidirectional unit↔analyzers link being the one deviation from strict layering.

Two of the five source zones (analyzers and analyzers-2) have significant bidirectional imports totaling 22 cross-imports, making them the dominant coupling hotspot in the package; resolving this by consolidating or drawing an explicit interface boundary would raise the average cohesion score meaningfully.

With 16 entry points, the 'analyzers' zone has a large public surface; a dedicated public.ts barrel (following the project's package convention) would make intentional exports explicit and prevent accidental API surface growth.
Fan-in hotspot: src/cli/output.ts receives calls from 13 files (high fan-in expected for this module type) — high-impact module, changes may have wide ripple effects
High coupling (0.57) — 3 imports target "static-analyzer-services"
High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Zone "analysis-pipeline" has files across 9 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

Add an src/analyzers/public.ts barrel as the single declared export surface for this zone, mirroring the package-level public.ts convention already used across all five packages; this caps the 16 entry points at a defined gate and makes undeclared cross-zone imports visible as convention violations.

Rename completion-reader.ts to enrich-reader.ts (or enrich-completion.ts) to complete the enrich-* naming cluster with enrich-batch.ts and enrich-config.ts; the current name breaks the discoverable naming pattern and obscures that these three files form a coherent AI enrichment subsystem.
Zone "cli-interface" has files across 7 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

Before: analysis-pipeline and core-algorithms import directly from cli/output.ts and cli/errors.ts. After: create src/shared/output.ts and src/shared/errors.ts containing the exported symbols; cli/output.ts and cli/errors.ts re-export from src/shared/; upstream zones import from src/shared/ — CLI remains a leaf consumer with zero inbound production imports and the 17 upward dependency edges become legitimate downward imports.
Zone "core-algorithms" has files across 11 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

Rename the zone to reflect its actual mixed content (e.g., 'algorithm-foundation') and enforce the name in architecture docs; the current name 'core-algorithms' causes reviewers to overlook misclassified non-algorithm files (workspace.ts, mcp.ts, branch-work-store.ts) because the name implies homogeneous algorithmic content.

core-algorithms has the worst cohesion/coupling ratio in the package (0.56 cohesion, 0.44 coupling) — it is the highest-fragility zone by metric and should be the primary stabilization target; all other refactoring work risks increasing this zone's instability further until its boundary is fixed.

Fixture coverage gap: both existing fixtures (remix-app, small-ts-project) are TypeScript-only. Add a plain JavaScript fixture project to ensure inventory, import, and zone detection code paths are exercised against JS files without TypeScript-specific syntax, since the analyzers must support both.

The perfect isolation of this zone (cohesion 1.0, coupling 0.0) is an implicit convention with no enforcement. Document it as the explicit target state for all test zones in PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md or an ADR so that accidental imports from/into fixture files trigger a documented rule violation rather than a silent metric regression.

analyzers-2 has a 15:7 import asymmetry with analysis-pipeline and participates in a three-way cycle (unit→analyzers→analyzers-2→unit via 2 return imports); consolidating analyzers-2 into analysis-pipeline would eliminate this cycle and reduce coupling

Excluding the two violations, all cross-zone import edges flow strictly downward (fixtures → test → domain → cli), confirming the intended layered architecture is largely intact and the codebase is close to a fully clean dependency graph.
Production code in two zones imports from the e2e test zone; shared test helpers should be moved to a non-test package (e.g., src/test-utils/) to eliminate production→test dependencies

Source zones importing from test zones (analyzers → e2e: 2, unit → e2e: 2) combined with the confirmed circular dependency count (2) strongly suggests these cross-boundary edges are the circular dependency chains — fixing them resolves both the layering violation and the circularity in one change.

Three-zone circular import cycle detected: core-algorithms → analysis-pipeline → analyzers-2 → core-algorithms; circular zone-level dependencies prevent clean layering and must be broken by extracting the shared interface

cli-interface functions as a shared output-formatting hub (88 total inbound calls across 2 upstream zones) despite being a leaf consumer by classification; a dedicated output-utilities zone should be extracted to make this responsibility explicit

Circular dependency: core-algorithms and e2e zone mutually import from each other (2 imports each direction) — circular production↔test coupling must be resolved by extracting shared test utilities to a non-test location
unit→cli (4 imports) violates the four-tier dependency hierarchy defined in CLAUDE.md — foundational algorithm code must not import from the CLI presentation layer

These 12 imports almost certainly represent shared output-formatting helpers or error types that belong in a foundation layer; until moved, every change to those CLI utilities risks breaking the analysis pipeline

src/analyzers/claude-client.ts bypasses @n-dx/llm-client (the designated foundation-layer vendor abstraction) and imports the Anthropic SDK directly from within the domain layer; this creates a second untracked LLM vendor coupling path and undermines the architectural guarantee that vendor lock-in is isolated to the foundation layer.
God function: registerMcpTools in src/cli/mcp.ts calls 36 unique functions — consider decomposing into smaller, focused functions

This is the only zone in the package that is fragile by both metrics simultaneously (cohesion 0.43 AND coupling 0.57). It should be treated as the highest-priority zone restructuring target: move src/schema/validate.ts to its correct source zone and separate e2e, integration, and unit test files into type-appropriate zones to drive both metrics toward the test-fixture-projects profile (cohesion 1.0, coupling 0.0).

Zone name 'integration-validation-tests' actively conceals that the zone also contains e2e and unit test files plus a production source module. Rename to something like 'mixed-test-zone' or split it — the misleading name is an obstacle to architectural remediation because it makes the zone appear coherent in documentation while the metrics show the opposite.

Cohesion 0.54 / coupling 0.46 puts this zone one misplaced file away from the fragile quadrant (cohesion < 0.5 AND coupling > 0.5). Extracting src/util/paths.ts to a shared foundation layer (already recommended) will move both metrics in the right direction before the zone degrades further.

Zone name 'static-analyzer-services' misleads — the suffix 'services' implies injectable or stateful objects, but all files are pure stateless analyzer functions. Rename to 'static-analyzers' or 'analyzer-utilities' to match actual content and avoid implying a service-layer pattern that does not exist.
